lessons.title: Preparation and Properties of Common Gases

content.body:
Key laboratory gases:

Hydrogen (H₂):
→ Zn + H₂SO₄ → ZnSO₄ + H₂↑
Test: burning splint → squeaky pop
Properties: lightest gas, flammable, colourless

Oxygen (O₂):
→ 2H₂O₂ → 2H₂O + O₂ (MnO₂ catalyst)
Test: glowing splint relights
Properties: supports combustion, colourless, odourless

CO₂:
→ CaCO₃ + HCl → CaCl₂ + H₂O + CO₂↑
Test: turns limewater milky
Properties: denser than air, extinguishes flames, acidic

content.title: More Gases
content.body:
Chlorine (Cl₂):
→ MnO₂ + 4HCl → MnCl₂ + 2H₂O + Cl₂
Test: bleaches damp litmus paper
Properties: yellow-green, toxic, pungent, heavier than air

Ammonia (NH₃):
→ Ca(OH)₂ + 2NH₄Cl → CaCl₂ + 2H₂O + 2NH₃
Test: turns damp red litmus blue; forms white smoke with HCl
Properties: pungent, colourless, less dense than air, alkaline

content.title: Collection Methods
content.body:
Over water: for insoluble gases (H₂, O₂)
Upward displacement of air: for denser gases (CO₂, Cl₂)
Downward displacement of air: for lighter gases (H₂, NH₃)

questions.question: The test for hydrogen gas uses a
questions.explanation: Hydrogen burns with a squeaky pop when a lit splint is applied

questions.question: Oxygen is tested using a
questions.explanation: A glowing (not burning) splint relights in the presence of oxygen

questions.question: CO₂ turns limewater
questions.explanation: CO₂ + Ca(OH)₂ → CaCO₃↓ + H₂O. Insoluble CaCO₃ makes limewater look milky

questions.question: Ammonia gas turns damp red litmus
questions.explanation: NH₃ dissolves in water forming NH₄OH (alkaline) → turns red litmus blue

questions.question: Hydrogen is collected over water because
questions.explanation: Gas collection over water works for gases that are insoluble in water (H₂, O₂)

questions.question: CO₂ extinguishes flames because
options: it is denser than air and doesn't support combustion
questions.answer: it is denser than air and doesn't support combustion
questions.explanation: CO₂: non-flammable, denser than air — blankets fire cutting off oxygen supply

questions.question: Chlorine bleaches damp litmus. This is because
options: it forms HClO (hypochlorous acid) which oxidises the dye
questions.answer: it forms HClO (hypochlorous acid) which oxidises the dye
questions.explanation: Cl₂+H₂O→HCl+HClO; hypochlorous acid is a powerful oxidising bleach

questions.question: Oxygen is prepared in the lab from
questions.explanation: 2H₂O₂ → 2H₂O + O₂↑ catalysed by MnO₂ (manganese dioxide)

questions.question: Ammonia is less dense than air and collected by
questions.explanation: Gas lighter than air rises — collect by downward displacement (gas pushes air down out of collection vessel)

questions.question: White smoke formed when ammonia meets HCl is
questions.explanation: NH₃(g) + HCl(g) → NH₄Cl(s) — dense white smoke of tiny ammonium chloride particles

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

content.body:
Boyle's Law (constant temperature):
P₁V₁ = P₂V₂
Pressure × Volume = constant
(P inversely proportional to V)

Charles' Law (constant pressure):
V₁/T₁ = V₂/T₂
Volume/Temperature = constant (T in Kelvin!)
K = °C + 273

Ideal Gas Law:
PV = nRT
P in Pa, V in m³, n = moles, R=8.314 J/mol/K, T in K

At STP: P=101,325 Pa, T=273K, 1 mol gas = 22.4 L

content.title: Boyle's Law Example
content.body:
Gas at 2 atm, volume 5L. Pressure increased to 5 atm at same temperature.
P₁V₁ = P₂V₂
2×5 = 5×V₂
V₂ = 2L

content.title: Charles' Law Example
content.body:
Gas at 20°C (293K), volume 6L. Heated to 80°C (353K).
V₁/T₁ = V₂/T₂
6/293 = V₂/353
V₂ = 6×353/293 = 7.23L

questions.question: Boyle's Law states that at constant temperature, pressure and volume are
questions.explanation: Boyle's Law: P∝1/V. Double the pressure → half the volume (same temperature)

questions.question: Convert 27°C to Kelvin
Gas: P₁=3atm, V₁=4L. Compress at constant T to V₂=2L. P₂ =
questions.explanation: P₁V₁=P₂V₂: 3×4=P₂×2, P₂=6atm

questions.question: Charles' Law states volume is directly proportional to
questions.explanation: Charles' Law: V∝T (K). Gas expands when heated at constant pressure

questions.question: Gas at 300K, volume 5L. Heated to 600K at constant pressure. Volume =
questions.explanation: V₁/T₁=V₂/T₂: 5/300=V₂/600, V₂=10L

questions.question: Ideal gas law: PV=nRT. Units of R =
questions.explanation: R = 8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹ — universal gas constant

questions.question: At STP, 1 mole of ideal gas occupies approximately
questions.explanation: Standard Temperature and Pressure (0°C, 1atm): 1 mol gas = 22.4 litres

questions.question: Real gases deviate from ideal behaviour most at
questions.explanation: At high P: molecules too close (intermolecular forces matter). Low T: kinetic energy low (interactions significant)

questions.question: Moles of gas in 5.6L at STP =
Combined gas law (P₁V₁/T₁ = P₂V₂/T₂). P₁=1atm, V₁=10L, T₁=300K, P₂=2atm, T₂=600K. V₂=

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Gases. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Gases concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of chemistry.
Core Theory

This section of Gases builds on foundational knowledge. In chemistry, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 3.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Gases. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 3.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Gases. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.
Common examination questions on Gases include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review
You have now covered all lessons in Gases. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

questions.question: What is the formula for calculating moles?
questions.explanation: Number of moles = mass (g) ÷ molar mass (g/mol). This is the fundamental mole equation.

questions.question: Which gas is produced when zinc reacts with dilute sulphuric acid?
questions.explanation: Zn + H₂SO₄ → ZnSO₄ + H₂. Metals above hydrogen in the reactivity series displace hydrogen from dilute acids.

questions.question: What is a covalent bond?
options: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.answer: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.explanation: In a covalent bond, two atoms share one or more pairs of electrons. Common in non-metal compounds like H₂O, CO₂, CH₄.

questions.question: What does the rate of reaction measure?
options: How fast reactants are converted to products
The energy released in a reaction
questions.answer: How fast reactants are converted to products
questions.explanation: Rate of reaction = change in concentration (or amount) of reactant/product per unit time.

questions.question: What is the effect of a catalyst on a reaction?
questions.explanation: A catalyst increases reaction rate by providing an alternative pathway with lower activation energy. It is not consumed.

questions.question: Which type of organic compound has the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂?
questions.explanation: Alkanes have the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂. They are saturated hydrocarbons with only single C-C bonds.

questions.question: What is the first ionisation energy?
options: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
Energy released when an electron is added
questions.answer: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
questions.explanation: First ionisation energy: X(g) → X⁺(g) + e⁻. It is the energy needed to remove the outermost electron from a gaseous atom.

questions.question: What is Le Chatelier's Principle?
options: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
Temperature has no effect on equilibrium
questions.answer: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
questions.explanation: Le Chatelier's Principle: A system at equilibrium responds to a change (temperature, pressure, concentration) by shifting to counteract it.

questions.question: What does 'exothermic' mean?
options: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.answer: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.explanation: Exothermic reactions release energy (ΔH < 0). Products have less energy than reactants. Examples: combustion, neutralisation.

questions.question: What is Boyle's Law?
options: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.answer: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.explanation: Boyle's Law: P ∝ 1/V at constant temperature. Doubling pressure halves volume (for ideal gases).

questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

questions.question: What is the formula for calculating moles?
questions.explanation: Number of moles = mass (g) ÷ molar mass (g/mol). This is the fundamental mole equation.

questions.question: Which gas is produced when zinc reacts with dilute sulphuric acid?
questions.explanation: Zn + H₂SO₄ → ZnSO₄ + H₂. Metals above hydrogen in the reactivity series displace hydrogen from dilute acids.

questions.question: What is a covalent bond?
options: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.answer: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.explanation: In a covalent bond, two atoms share one or more pairs of electrons. Common in non-metal compounds like H₂O, CO₂, CH₄.

questions.question: What does the rate of reaction measure?
options: How fast reactants are converted to products
The energy released in a reaction
questions.answer: How fast reactants are converted to products
questions.explanation: Rate of reaction = change in concentration (or amount) of reactant/product per unit time.

questions.question: What is the effect of a catalyst on a reaction?
questions.explanation: A catalyst increases reaction rate by providing an alternative pathway with lower activation energy. It is not consumed.

questions.question: Which type of organic compound has the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂?
questions.explanation: Alkanes have the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂. They are saturated hydrocarbons with only single C-C bonds.

questions.question: What is the first ionisation energy?
options: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
Energy released when an electron is added
questions.answer: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
questions.explanation: First ionisation energy: X(g) → X⁺(g) + e⁻. It is the energy needed to remove the outermost electron from a gaseous atom.

questions.question: What is Le Chatelier's Principle?
options: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
Temperature has no effect on equilibrium
questions.answer: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
questions.explanation: Le Chatelier's Principle: A system at equilibrium responds to a change (temperature, pressure, concentration) by shifting to counteract it.

questions.question: What does 'exothermic' mean?
options: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.answer: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.explanation: Exothermic reactions release energy (ΔH < 0). Products have less energy than reactants. Examples: combustion, neutralisation.

questions.question: What is Boyle's Law?
options: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.answer: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.explanation: Boyle's Law: P ∝ 1/V at constant temperature. Doubling pressure halves volume (for ideal gases).
